name: Tamarindo
summary: Tamarindo is one of Costa Rica's safest beach towns, with a mature tourism infrastructure and strong security presence. While petty crime exists and tourist-focused scams occur, violent crime against visitors is rare and the surf town's community-oriented culture provides natural safety.
quickVerdict: Tamarindo is widely considered one of Costa Rica's safest destinations for tourists. This Pacific coast surf town benefits from excellent infrastructure, visible police presence, and a well-established expat community. Most safety concerns involve preventable petty theft and overcharging scams rather than serious crime. With basic precautions like securing valuables and avoiding poorly lit areas at night, visitors can safely enjoy Tamarindo's beaches, nightlife, and outdoor activities.

neighborhoods.name: Central Tamarindo Beach Area
neighborhoods.description: The main tourist zone with excellent lighting, regular police patrols, and high pedestrian traffic. Hotels, restaurants, and shops create natural surveillance. Safest area day and night.

neighborhoods.name: Playa Langosta
neighborhoods.description: Upscale residential area just 5 minutes from central Tamarindo. Quieter with luxury hotels and condos. Well-lit streets and security-conscious community make it very safe.

neighborhoods.name: Tamarindo Heights/Hilltop Area
neighborhoods.description: Elevated residential area with ocean views. Good lighting and close proximity to main areas. Popular with families and couples seeking quieter accommodations.

neighborhoods.name: Main Strip (Calle Central)
neighborhoods.description: Busy commercial area with restaurants and nightlife. Generally safe due to activity but watch for pickpockets in crowded areas, especially during peak season.

neighborhoods.name: Backstreets Behind Main Strip
neighborhoods.description: Poorly lit residential streets away from tourist areas. Less foot traffic at night makes these areas riskier after dark. Exercise caution and use taxis when possible.

neighborhoods.name: Estuary/North End Near River
neighborhoods.description: Remote area where the river meets the ocean. Poor lighting and isolation make this area risky at night. Wildlife concerns include crocodiles in the estuary waters.

scams.name: Overpriced Taxi Scams
scams.description: Taxi drivers claim broken meters or quote inflated prices, especially to tourists. Some pirate taxis operate without proper licenses.
scams.howToAvoid: Always insist on using the meter (called Maria), verify taxi license plates match door numbers, and ask your hotel for estimated fare prices beforehand.

scams.name: Flat Tire Assistance Scam
scams.description: Someone signals that you have a flat tire, then accomplices steal items from your car while you're distracted checking the supposed problem.
scams.howToAvoid: If warned about car problems, drive to a well-lit public place like a gas station before stopping. Don't accept help from strangers on isolated roads.

scams.name: Fake Tour Companies
scams.description: Unlicensed tour operators offer cheap excursions but provide substandard service, safety issues, or cancel entirely after taking deposits.
scams.howToAvoid: Book with established companies that have proper licenses and insurance. Check Google Reviews and ask your hotel for recommendations. Avoid paying full amounts upfront.

scams.name: ATM Card Skimming
scams.description: Criminals install card readers on ATMs or offer to help with transactions while using skimming devices to steal card information and PINs.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs inside banks or hotels when possible. Cover your PIN entry and check for unusual attachments on card slots. Decline help from strangers at ATMs.

soloFemale.overview: Tamarindo is considered one of Costa Rica's safest destinations for solo female travelers. The established tourism infrastructure, visible security presence, and community atmosphere provide good support systems. Many female-only hostels and women-friendly accommodations are available. While harassment is uncommon, basic precautions are still recommended.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed accommodations in central areas or female-only hostels for added security and community
Join group activities like surf lessons or tours to meet other travelers and enhance safety through numbers
Use licensed taxis at night rather than walking alone, especially when returning from bars or restaurants
Limit alcohol consumption and never leave drinks unattended in nightlife areas
Share your itinerary with someone back home and check in regularly, especially when doing solo activities

nightSafety.overview: Tamarindo's nightlife is generally safe with good lighting and police presence in main areas. The town doesn't shut down after sunset, maintaining natural surveillance through restaurant and bar activity. However, caution is needed in poorly lit backstreets and when consuming alcohol.
tips: Stick to main strip areas with good lighting and avoid backstreets near the river or estuary after dark
Travel in groups when bar-hopping and use licensed taxis for transportation between venues
Watch alcohol consumption carefully as happy hour deals can lead to overindulgence and increased vulnerability
Never leave drinks unattended and only accept drinks directly from bartenders
Have your accommodation contact information written down in case you become separated from your group

transport.metro: No metro system exists in Tamarindo. Local transportation relies on taxis, private shuttles, and rental vehicles.
transport.rideshare: Uber operates in Tamarindo but with limited availability. DiDi is also present as an alternative. Service can be unreliable with few drivers, so don't rely solely on ride-sharing apps.
transport.taxis: Official taxis are red or orange with yellow triangles and license numbers. Always insist on using the meter (Maria) or agree on fares beforehand. Many pirate taxis operate - verify official markings before getting in.
transport.tips: Public buses to other destinations are safe but watch belongings during peak hours. Private shuttles offer reliable airport transfers. Rental cars work well for exploring nearby beaches but parking in town is limited. Bicycles are popular for short distances but avoid riding at night due to poor lighting.

customs: Greet with a warm smile, firm handshake, or light cheek kiss among friends - personal warmth is highly valued in Costa Rican culture
Embrace Tico Time - arriving 15-30 minutes late to social gatherings is normal and acceptable, though be punctual for tours and official appointments
Use Pura Vida liberally as greeting, farewell, or expression of thanks - it embodies the positive, laid-back Costa Rican philosophy of life

health.overview: Costa Rica has excellent healthcare standards ranking first in Latin America. Tamarindo has several private clinics for basic care, with the nearest hospital in Santa Cruz 40 minutes away. For serious emergencies, quality hospitals in San Jose are accessible within a few hours.
health.water: Tap water in Tamarindo is generally safe to drink and regularly tested. Most tourists have no issues, though those with sensitive stomachs may prefer bottled water initially.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required for Tamarindo. Standard travel vaccines like Hepatitis A/B and typhoid are recommended. Yellow fever vaccination required only if arriving from certain African or South American countries.
health.altitude: Tamarindo is at sea level with year-round temperatures between 80-95°F. High humidity requires staying hydrated. Use DEET-based mosquito repellent especially at dawn and dusk to prevent dengue and other mosquito-borne illnesses.

emergency.touristPolice: 2258-1008 (San Jose) or report via SOSAFE app
emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy San Jose: +506-2519-2000 (after hours emergency: +506-8863-4895). Local medical services: Dr. Piloto clinic +506-8835-8074, Beachside Clinic Huacas +506-2653-9911.

faq.a: Yes, Tamarindo is considered one of Costa Rica's safest beach destinations. It has excellent tourism infrastructure, regular police patrols, and low violent crime rates. Most issues involve preventable petty theft rather than serious safety concerns.

Tamarindo is generally safe at night in well-lit main areas where restaurants and bars operate. However, avoid poorly lit backstreets and areas near the estuary after dark. Use taxis for transportation and travel in groups when possible.

faq.q: Is Tamarindo safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, Tamarindo is excellent for solo female travelers with many women-only hostels, established tourist infrastructure, and a community atmosphere. Exercise normal precautions like staying in central areas and joining group activities for added safety.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Tamarindo?
faq.a: Avoid poorly lit backstreets behind the main strip and the estuary/north end area near where the river meets the ocean, especially at night. These areas have less foot traffic and security presence.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Tamarindo?
faq.a: Local buses are safe but watch belongings during crowded times. Taxis are reliable when using official red/orange vehicles with yellow triangles. Private shuttles offer secure airport transfers. Uber operates but with limited availability.
